title: TBM 397: The Initiative Shape Activity (And a Favor to Ask)
questions: How do we characterize the shape of an initiative so we know what kind of work and governance it needs?
What level of coordination and sustained attention will this initiative require across teams?
Where is the greatest uncertainty and how early can we de-risk through incremental progress?
Which discovery approach and decision authority model best fit this initiative’s value timing, constraints, and delay sensitivity?
summary: The post provides a practical 5-minute workshop activity for teams to independently assess an initiative’s “shape” across multiple dimensions (coordination, time horizon, value timing, uncertainty, constraints, delay sensitivity, discovery approach, and decision authority). It’s aimed at leaders and product/engineering teams who want a more actionable way to surface ambiguity, drive alignment, and decide what to clarify or discover next. The author also invites participants to take the survey and feed back improvements for later integration into a prioritization tool.

title: TBM 387: Lenses
questions: Which lenses should we use in our product and ops planning, and when?
How do customer journey stages map to wardley proximity and capability evolution?
How should team boundaries and team interaction patterns be represented in a product strategy map?
How can we turn a complex visualization into actionable opportunities and options without getting stuck in diagramming?
search_terms: wardley mapping x axis y axis
team topologies X as a Service facilitation collaboration
summary: The post argues that effective product and ops work depends on using multiple “lenses” (customer journey, wardley mapping, team boundaries/interactions, opportunities/options, and execution) rather than relying on a single view. It’s aimed at product teams and operators who build strategy diagrams and need a practical way to connect dimensions and choose which lenses matter at the right time.

title: TBM 352: Funding, Planning, and Context
questions: How should we plan and fund teams when they have different stages, dependencies, and proximity to revenue?
What goes wrong when we apply one budgeting/goal-setting model across all teams?
How do effort–value curves change how we should govern long-running or high-uncertainty work?
Should tech/product be treated as the business or as an enabling function for planning purposes?
search_terms: funding teams by stage and work shape
hill climbing model for organizational growth
summary: This post argues that planning and funding in product and tech organizations must be context-aware, because teams differ in stage, work shape, dependencies, and how value accrues. It provides a framework for diagnosing team contexts (including effort–value curves and exploration vs optimization) and highlights anti-patterns that emerge when organizations use rigid, certainty-seeking governance. It’s aimed at leaders and product/engineering managers who need a practical way to design funding and planning that stays legible without forcing a single monoprocess.

title: TBM 346: Broken Record Models
questions: What aspects of my product area will remain true five years from now?
Which stable drivers should guide how we allocate time and energy over quarters and years?
What strategic factors matter most for this product area, regardless of shifting tactics?
How can a shared language with partners improve continuity and reduce reactive decision-making?
search_terms: network effects first vs integrations depth
bet portfolio allocation for product teams
summary: This post argues that product managers should use durable “broken record” models—stable drivers and vocabulary—to align partners and stakeholders around long-term outcomes rather than reactive, trend-driven tactics. It provides examples of frameworks for partner integration and product evolution, emphasizing that while strategies and effort allocation shift over time, the underlying drivers should remain consistent. The guidance is aimed at PMs and product leaders managing partner ecosystems and cross-functional alignment.

title: Roadmapping/Bets AMA (John Cutler)
questions: How do you plan using bets rather than fixed roadmaps?
What success metrics should teams use to evaluate bets?
How can roadmaps be structured around measurable outcomes?
What practical frameworks or examples can help with roadmapping and metrics?
summary: This post announces a free AMA focused on roadmapping, bet-based planning, and choosing success metrics. It’s aimed at product, strategy, and planning practitioners who want practical guidance and examples (including materials like a mini book and Miro boards).

title: TBM 332: The Last Strategy Framework You'll Ever Need
questions: What questions should we answer first when building a strategy, and in what sequence?
How do we move from strategy frameworks as static artifacts to strategy as an interactive, team-latched process?
Why do strategy frameworks overlap yet still feel different, and how should we choose among them?
How do we make strategy decisions when we only have partial, low-confidence information?
search_terms: strategy kernel diagnosis guiding policy coherent actions
strategy narrative identity power inside organizations
summary: The post argues that strategy frameworks share a common underlying pattern (questions, tools, visualization, and deployment) and that the real difficulty of strategy comes from uncertainty and human organizational dynamics. It compares tool-heavy frameworks like Wardley Mapping and Zone to Win with decision-structure approaches like Rumelt’s strategy kernel, emphasizing how to choose and apply frameworks based on context. It’s aimed at product operations and teams trying to make strategy collaborative and actionable rather than a static, box-filling exercise.

title: TBM 325: Skeptoptimism – Thinking Slow, Acting Fast
questions: How do I think slow without falling into analysis paralysis?
What does it mean to plan through experimentation rather than abstract calculation?
How can I communicate skepticism and optimism so others don’t misread deep dives as pessimism or delay?
How do I ensure my team is truly aligned before I rely on their ability to pivot and recover?
summary: The post argues that “thinking slow, acting fast” should be practiced as active experimentation and experience, not passive deliberation. It’s aimed at leaders and team members who identify as skeptoptimists and want to navigate the tension between deep risk exploration and rapid execution, including how to communicate optimism so others don’t mistake it for waste or pessimism.

title: TBM 230: From Prioritization to Accountability (and Autonomy)
questions: What funding and accountability model should a product team use to ensure ROI and outcomes?
How can product leaders replace ceremonial prioritization with a system that closes the loop on results?
What milestones and metrics would build confidence in continued investment in a product team?
What game is your product playing inside the company: contractor-style delivery or startup-style investment?
search_terms: startup investor game for product teams
summary: The post argues for product leaders to define an investment and governance framework that ties funding to milestone outcomes rather than project lists. It contrasts “contractor game” internal development—where teams deliver promised work and then seek more headcount—with a “startup/investor game” that uses progressive, re-up funding to enable pivoting, scaling, or sunsetting. It’s aimed at leaders navigating efficiency, productivity, and ROI pressures who want greater autonomy through stronger accountability.

title: TBM 221: Strategy = Insights^Conviction
questions: Does a strategy need to be unexpected to be effective?
How can you tell what a company’s strategy really is from the outside?
What uncomfortable NOs and guiding policies enable an “obvious” go-to-market move?
What unique twist, timing, or sequencing makes a standard play succeed when others fail?
search_terms: strategy is choosing what not to do
how to explain strategy with a twist
summary: The post argues that while simple, obvious strategies can be effective, the real substance of strategy lies in the guiding policies and “uncomfortable NOs” that determine what gets deprioritized, funded, measured, and risked. It’s aimed at product and go-to-market leaders who want a sharper way to diagnose and communicate why a standard play works for their company—through unique insights, capabilities, sequencing, and coherent actions.

title: TBM 219: Follow the Money (People, Products, or Projects)
questions: Does your company fund people, products, or projects—and what does that mean in practice?
Why do enterprises default to project-based funding even when they talk about product thinking?
How does shifting from people/product investment to project funding change team behavior and outcomes?
What would it take to maintain long-term conviction while still meeting governance and accountability needs?
search_terms: people vs projects vs products funding
team retention and product success compounding
summary: The post contrasts three ways companies allocate resources—funding people, products, or projects—and argues that many enterprises default to project funding for control, accountability, and strategic alignment. It’s aimed at leaders and product/strategy practitioners who want to understand how funding units shape team incentives, long-term bets, and the risk of turning a high-performing product team into a deliverables-driven project machine.

title: TBM 218: Level, Depth, Time, and Frame
questions: How do I balance being high-level with being specific without losing strategic coherence?
How often should I update strategy templates and roadmaps across different time horizons?
What strategy “frame” should I use when communicating with different stakeholders?
How do I align quarterly plans, annual goals, and multi-year strategy so they reinforce each other?
search_terms: strategy dimensions level specificity time frame
Rumelt strategy diagnosis guiding principles coherent actions
summary: The post proposes a practical way to think about strategy by mapping it to four dimensions: level, depth (specificity), time horizon, and frame. It’s aimed at product managers and strategists who feel pulled between vague big-picture thinking and demands for concrete details, and it helps them choose the right strategy expression and update cadence. It also offers a mental model for how strategy artifacts and refresh cycles can coexist across multiple time horizons.

title: TBM 204: 40 (Roadmap Item) Questions
questions: Where are we in the life cycle of this problem or opportunity, and how quickly can we make progress?
What is the risk profile, expected blast radius, and leverage of this proposed initiative?
How solution-specific are we, and should we be problem-, solution-, or opportunity-focused?
What domain does this problem fall into (simple/complicated/complex/chaotic), and what feedback loops do we need?
search_terms: blast radius and risk profile questions
problem vs solution vs opportunity focused
reversibility and pivot criteria for product bets
summary: This post provides a reusable set of multiple-choice questions for product managers to pressure-test roadmap bets across uncertainty, speed, risk, leverage, differentiation, and operational readiness. It’s designed for ongoing exploration rather than gatekeeping, encouraging learning from answers and jumping between related questions. The guidance is especially useful for teams trying to decide what to do next when the problem space is unclear.

title: TBM 7/52: Two Bets
questions: How should we structure bets to reduce uncertainty before outcomes?
What is the difference between estimating work completion and estimating time to meaningful progress?
Why do short iterative bets build more trust than big-batch projects?
How do outcome lag and leveling-off affect whether we should continue a bet?
search_terms: two bets team A team B
outcome lag weeks 5 dip weeks 7 8 leveling off
summary: The post contrasts two execution patterns for teams working on 1–3 month opportunities: short nested bets that reduce uncertainty early versus a single longer bet that delays outcomes. It argues that the key differentiator is not just speed or work completion, but how quickly teams generate meaningful progress, build trust, and create measurable lift. It is aimed at product and delivery leaders designing roadmaps, bets, and success metrics.

title: TBM 51/52: Shaky Foundations (and Planning Escalation)
questions: How do we break a cycle of churn, pivots, and reactivity in product execution?
Why do big-bang plans fail when the organization lacks a strong foundation?
What does it mean to operate in exploration mode without stalling progress?
How should startups and larger companies structure strategy and ways of working as layered foundations?
summary: This post argues that persistent churn and pivoting indicate a weak organizational foundation, which leads teams to manufacture certainty via big-bang plans or costly exploration. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to stabilize execution by slowing down, building layered foundations (strategy and ways of working), and shifting to smaller “think big, work small” efforts that enable learning. It also explains how these dynamics create predictable stress-driven camps and burnout cycles.

title: TBM 48/52: Hill Climbing
questions: How do we categorize our product opportunities into exploratory, low-hanging fruit, disciplined optimization, hard-to-win, and controlled-fall stages?
What does our portfolio of bets look like for a specific business lever (e.g., trustworthy data or situational awareness)?
Are we using the right approach for each stage, or are we treating all bets as if they were the same?
What should we intentionally not do because of technology shifts and the need to let A bets materialize?
search_terms: portfolio of bets A B C D E
trustworthy data lever ML alerting governance
summary: The post proposes a stage-based way to classify product opportunities (A–E) from exploratory hill-climbing to hard-to-win and controlled deprioritization. It argues that teams should adapt their approach based on where each bet sits, and use a lever-by-lever portfolio exercise to map bets across stages. It’s aimed at product and strategy leaders who want a practical framework for managing innovation, optimization, and technology shifts.

title: TBM 47/52: Prompts for Exploring Strategic Tensions and Polarities
questions: How can we use prompts to explore strategic tensions and polarities during annual planning?
Why do organizations avoid focus-inducing statements, and how does that affect planning?
What is a good facilitation approach for generating and refining strategy prompt answers?
How do we structure strategy decisions to account for counterintuitive tradeoffs and risky assumptions?
search_terms: focus as a threat political stalemate
turn the game into a game of
summary: The post provides strategy prompts designed to help teams navigate annual planning by explicitly surfacing strategic tensions and polarities. It argues that organizations often avoid such clarity due to internal politics, but that structured prompts can make planning more generative. It’s aimed at leaders and facilitators who want practical ways to run group exercises and refine strategy language without personalizing the exercise.

title: Plan on a Page Checklist
questions: What is our mission and high-level focus right now?
What is going well and what isn’t, and what is the key leverage point?
What should we accomplish in the next 3 months and 12 months?
What are our workstreams, initiatives, milestones, dependencies, non-goals, assumptions, and risks?
summary: This post provides a practical checklist for creating a “plan on a page” that aligns teams around mission, a clear diagnosis, time-bounded focus, and the workstreams that drive outcomes. It’s aimed at leaders and operators who need a lightweight but complete planning artifact, including a 12-month roadmap, milestones, dependencies, non-goals, assumptions, and risks—while avoiding premature commitment.

title: TBM 38/52: Navigating the Product Leadership Fog
questions: How do I handle the gap between corporate strategy and day-to-day plans when priorities keep changing?
What should I do when both strategic thinkers and get-it-done thinkers are frustrated and misaligned?
How do I rebuild trust and psychological safety when the team is in blame mode and people are leaving?
What does “good enough” strategy look like when we don’t have time to think strategically?
search_terms: degrading trust psychological safety product team
how to relieve teams from filling the void
summary: This post offers guidance for product leaders navigating organizational confusion during rapid growth and constant priority shifts. It frames the core issue as a “leadership fog” driven by degrading trust and psychological safety, and it distinguishes the different needs of strategic thinkers versus get-it-done thinkers. It’s aimed at leaders who feel pressure to produce perfect strategy and plan but need practical steps to restore trust, reduce blame, and create cadence with a good-enough strategy.

title: TBM 19/52: A Portfolio of Bets
questions: How do we balance our portfolio of bets across initiatives with different risk/reward profiles?
What’s the difference between opportunity risk and solution risk when planning experiments?
How should our roadmap’s “bet mix” change as a startup moves from early to later stages?
Are we hedging appropriately, or over-hedging our bets?
summary: The post argues that teams should plan roadmaps as a portfolio of bets, where each initiative has a distinct risk/reward profile and the mix should evolve with changing strategy and goals. It’s aimed at product and startup leaders who want a more intentional way to think about experimentation, opportunity vs solution risk, and how portfolio balance shifts from early to later stages.

title: TBM 48/52: Premature Convergence
questions: Why do product teams converge too early instead of exploring options?
What organizational incentives and structures increase the risk of premature convergence?
How can we distinguish a healthy bias for action from premature convergence?
What practical interventions help teams regain optionality and avoid lock-in?
search_terms: bias for action vs premature convergence
North Star framework and OKRs inputs flywheels
sunk cost bias in product planning
summary: The post explains premature convergence as a common product and organizational failure mode where teams lock into directions too early, reducing optionality and harming outcomes. It’s aimed at product, engineering, and leadership teams who want to improve decision quality by distinguishing experimentation from lock-in and by adopting practices like decision reviews, structured divergence, and time-boxed discovery.

title: TBM 46/52: Annual Planning (Wicked Problem)
questions: Why can’t product teams do more continuous planning instead of annual planning?
What makes annual planning a wicked problem in real organizations?
How do execution mode, feedback latency, and output-centricity undermine strategy work?
What small experiments could replace the annual planning ritual with more frequent planning cycles?
search_terms: reduce feedback loop latency product planning
summary: The post argues that while continuous planning is theoretically achievable and beneficial, annual planning persists because it functions like a wicked problem shaped by organizational constraints. It helps product and strategy leaders understand the real-world drivers—execution mode, slow feedback, output pressure, budgeting and executive timing—that make frequent planning difficult, and suggests experimenting with recurring planning time blocks.

title: TBM 41b/52: Suitably Detailed Roadmap Items
questions: How do you structure a one-year rolling product roadmap with the right level of detail at each time horizon?
Which metrics best capture progress for accountants’ monthly reconciliation workflows?
What roadmap initiatives (model improvements, UX, education, collaboration) most effectively reduce reconciliation time and errors?
How should teams prioritize among multiple potential options for near-future workflow improvements?
summary: The post outlines how to build a one-year rolling product roadmap for a specific user group (Tier 3 accountants) using progressive disclosure: high-level intent for far-future work, measurable targets for near-future work, and prioritized options for immediate execution. It emphasizes outcome metrics such as 90th percentile reconciliation time and proposes initiatives spanning AI categorization improvements, UX changes for scanning and clarification, and organizational research on collaboration patterns.

title: TBM 39/52: Monthly Strategy Prompt Exercise
questions: What signals should we expect to increase, and what implications do they have for our strategy?
Where might threats emerge, and how would our assumptions need to change if they do?
What tactical pivots can we make without abandoning our core strategic focus?
What capability or internal bottleneck should we strengthen next, and how will we experiment to improve it?
search_terms: bottleneck shift from shipping to learning
summary: The post proposes a monthly strategy prompt routine to help teams stay grounded during and after annual planning cycles. It is aimed at product, strategy, and leadership teams who want a lightweight way to capture expectations, threats, tactical pivots, and capability bottlenecks while building a reviewable history for faster learning and assumption updates.

title: TBM 37/52: Bet Taxonomy (Roll Your Own)
questions: How do we categorize product initiatives so we can tailor how we work, decide, and measure progress?
What initiative attributes should determine risk thinking and the definition of done?
How can we avoid copying another company’s process while still standardizing effectively?
When should teams vary discovery, kickoff structure, PRD formats, and success metrics by initiative type?
search_terms: resist sprint normalization quarters meeting cadence
summary: The post argues that product teams should not apply a single uniform process to every kind of initiative, because different bets imply different decision rules, risk profiles, and progress signals. It recommends creating an in-house “bet taxonomy” that categorizes initiatives by the details that drive how teams work, then defining category-specific working agreements and metrics. It’s aimed at product and product-ops leaders trying to improve how teams plan, discover, and execute across varied work types.

title: TBM 20/52: A Product Super-Skill (Balancing Divergence and Convergence)
questions: Is my organization converging too early or taking too long to converge—and how can both happen at once?
How do we avoid a 'there-but-not-there' strategy that doesn’t guide decisions?
What practices help manage uncertainty without rushing to plans or getting stuck in endless exploration?
How should we time decisions and agreements to improve execution?
summary: The post explores how product teams and organizations get stuck in a harmful loop between premature convergence on plans and delayed convergence on understanding. It’s aimed at product leaders and practitioners who need a practical way to balance divergence and convergence, rebuild trust, and manage uncertainty through learning-oriented strategy rather than faux certainty. It also emphasizes improving the timing and flow of decisions so agreement and execution happen at the right moments.

title: TBM 7/52: Risk Checklist Activity
questions: How can our team talk about risk and uncertainty in a way that leads to better planning?
What initiative risks are we missing when our risk section is only a few generic bullets?
How do we ensure our risk checklist is specific, actionable, and not just theater?
How can we validate and improve a risk checklist using past projects?
search_terms: pressure test risk questions with historical efforts
summary: The post proposes a practical, team-run activity for creating and using a checklist of initiative risk questions to improve how teams discuss uncertainty. It is aimed at product and project teams that currently treat risk as a one-time PRD section or focus narrowly on schedule risks. The guidance emphasizes specificity, cross-functional input, historical validation, continuous revision, and avoiding performative review dynamics.

title: TBM 5/52: The Promise, The Path, The Pressure
questions: What promise is our product making to customers, and how do we phrase it clearly?
What is our path to sustainable growth, and which levers (channels, segments, loops, partnerships, costs) matter most?
How do we compare to competitors and the status quo on key capabilities?
Where do we need to maintain our position versus work to improve?
search_terms: business model canvas for growth ideas
summary: The post offers a lightweight facilitation exercise for product teams to clarify product strategy using three prompts: the promise to customers, the path to sustainable growth, and competitive pressure (including the status quo). It is aimed at product managers and growth leaders who want a practical way to align teams quickly and prepare for strategy workshops.

title: TBM 4/52: Think Big, Work Small (Part 2)
questions: How does my team work today—big, small, or big-defined with small execution?
What incentives are keeping us stuck in a dysfunctional delivery pattern?
How can we align on a mission while still leaving room for learning and feedback?
Where should we start: smaller iterations, a clearer big-picture mission, or less prescriptive planning?
summary: This post contrasts common team operating modes—working big, working small, and hybrid approaches—and explains why each can produce predictable dysfunction. It argues for a “thinking big, working small” model that couples a coherent, outcome-oriented mission with learning-first, broad-stroke execution. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to improve delivery effectiveness and break incentive-driven cycles.

title: TBM 2.1/52: Continuous Roadmapping
questions: How can we avoid the waste caused by rushed, big-batch roadmap planning?
What does continuous roadmapping look like in practice for product teams?
How do we keep a roadmap always up to date without overplanning or estimation?
What should replace feature-count roadmaps when releases are frequent?
search_terms: roadmap as a set of bets
summary: The post argues that traditional big-batch roadmap planning and reactive whack-a-mole prioritization both waste money and attention. It proposes continuous roadmapping using a small, always-filled board of time-horizon “bets,” grounded in a north star metric, persistent goals, and outcome-oriented one-pagers. It’s aimed at product leaders and agile teams who want an always up-to-date roadmap without overcommitting or relying on heavy estimation.

title: TBM 48/53: One Roadmap
questions: Should we split customer-facing and internal/infrastructure work into separate roadmaps?
How do we get executives to understand and prioritize unsexy infrastructure work?
What causes shadow roadmaps and how can we prevent them?
How can engineering leaders build a strong business case for enabling work?
search_terms: how to present infrastructure roadmap to executives
summary: The post argues for maintaining a single, integrated roadmap rather than splitting customer-facing and non-customer-facing work. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders who present roadmaps to executives and want infrastructure/enabling work to be valued, prioritized, and understood. The core message is that visibility and business-case communication prevent shadow roadmaps, reduce debt buildup, and protect trust.

title: TBM 46/53: Think Big, Work Small
questions: How can a team support both big-picture thinking and small iterative delivery?
What causes teams to iterate to nowhere instead of making progress toward a coherent strategy?
Why do some organizations diminish strategy or working small, and how can they correct that?
How do you avoid splitting the organization into “think big” and “work small” groups?
search_terms: learning as fast as you ship
agile used only to ship faster
summary: The post argues that effective product teams must do both big-picture strategy and small iterative execution, using thin slices to de-risk and learn as they ship. It explains why teams often over-index on one side—either dismissing strategy or treating agile as mere faster shipping—and warns against organizing people into separate “think big” vs “work small” groups. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to improve how they plan, iterate, and create tangible progress together.

title: TBM 42/53: The Annual Planning Dance
questions: Is the annual planning dance the best or only way to align strategy and budgets?
Where does annual planning typically break down—during the handoff from leadership to departments?
How can teams avoid premature convergence and the messy middle during strategy deployment?
What planning approach works better than a painful 2+ month annual cycle?
search_terms: strategy handoff between leadership and teams
summary: The post examines why annual planning often becomes a high-drama, siloed diverge-converge process with negotiation and deadlines, and argues it doesn’t have to be inevitable. It’s aimed at leaders and product/strategy practitioners who want a better way to translate vague top-level direction into workable departmental plans by improving the early handoff and enabling shared sense-making. The core focus is preventing the 'messy middle' and 'premature convergence' through a 'start together' approach.

title: TBM 35/53: Basic Prioritization Questions (and When to Converge on a Solution)
questions: How much solutioning should we do before starting an effort?
Which uncertainties matter most right now, and how confident are we about each?
When should we experiment to de-risk instead of settling on a solution?
What happens to risk if we converge on a solution early?
search_terms: when to converge on a solution
summary: This post argues that product teams should delay solutioning until they’ve clarified key uncertainties, especially opportunity value and opportunity size. It offers a practical set of prioritization questions and confidence-based reasoning to decide when to experiment versus when to converge on a solution. It’s aimed at product managers, designers, and engineers who want better alignment and lower risk in early product development.

title: TBM 27/53: The Lure of New Features and Products
questions: Why do teams keep shipping new features instead of fixing underlying product problems?
How do incentives and optics shape what makes it onto the roadmap?
What happens when core adoption, customer outcome, or value proposition issues are not addressed?
How can leaders shift incentives toward optimizing existing product areas?
search_terms: least resistance path in product strategy
fixing underlying product issues vs shipping features
summary: This post examines why B2B teams often default to a “feature factory” approach that prioritizes shipping new, marketable capabilities over messy optimization work. It argues that the behavior is driven by incentives and optics—visible wins are easier to achieve—leading to roadmap gaps around adoption, customer outcomes, and value proposition problems. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to realign strategy and execution toward harder, underlying fixes.

title: TBM 21/53: "Vision" and Prescriptive Roadmaps
questions: How do you communicate product vision so people trust it and feel commitment?
Why do feature factories persist even when teams do good work?
What does it mean to sell outcomes instead of selling future features?
How can an outcome-oriented roadmap avoid getting reset by leadership overcorrection?
summary: The post argues that “product vision” often functions as a trust and commitment signal, so many organizations respond better to prescriptive roadmaps that clearly state what will be built. It explains how incentives and organizational optics can entrench feature-factory behavior, and why outcome-focused teams may still struggle without actively selling outcomes and broadcasting real results. It’s aimed at product leaders and product managers trying to shift from feature-centric roadmaps to outcome-oriented planning without losing organizational buy-in.

title: TBM 14/53: 1s and 3s
questions: How can we connect day-to-day work to company-level goals across multiple time horizons?
What time-scale framework helps teams avoid over-precision while still maintaining coherence?
Where do our plans become unclear between sprint work and executive-level strategy?
How do we ensure work is traceable end-to-end rather than siloed by team or sprint?
search_terms: bet traceability near to long term
summary: The post proposes a simple planning framework using connected 1-3 ranges of time (hours through decades) to help teams link their immediate work to longer-term company bets. It is aimed at teams struggling with alignment and coherence, especially in the often unclear 1-3 month to 1-3 quarter “messy middle.”

title: BM 1/53: The Empty Roadmap
questions: What happens to our business metrics if we stop shipping and only handle production issues?
Which assumptions are we making about how our work contributes to current outcomes?
How quickly does inaction show up in our market, and what would surprise us over a year?
How do we create a safe environment for teams to explore counterfactual futures?
summary: The post proposes an “empty roadmap” exercise where teams imagine stopping shipping new work to see how business dashboards might change over months. It’s aimed at product and cross-functional leaders who want to surface assumptions, challenge causal storytelling, and reframe planning around the opportunity and long-term slow-burn effects. The author emphasizes that the exercise requires freedom and psychological safety to explore counterfactual futures without judgment.
